back_translation: Later, in the reign of King Ahasuerus — that is, the Ahasuerus who reigned over the one hundred twenty-seven provinces, from India as far as Cush —
at that time, when King Ahasuerus was seated upon the royal throne of his kingdom at the citadel-city of Susa,
in the third year of his reign, he held a banquet for all his officials and servants; the army commanders of Persia and Media, the high nobles, and the governors of the provinces were before him.
He displayed the wealth of his glorious kingdom and the splendid magnificence of his great majesty for many days, as many as one hundred eighty days.
When those days were completed, the king held a seven-day banquet for all the people present in the citadel-city of Susa, from the greatest to the least, in the garden court of the royal palace.

There were curtains of fine white linen and blue, fastened with cords of fine linen and purple thread looped on silver rings on marble pillars; there were couches of gold and silver set on a floor inlaid with bahat stone, marble, mother-of-pearl, and dark stone.
The drinks were served in vessels of gold, each of a different design, and there was royal wine in abundance according to the king's generosity.
The drinking was according to the royal order that no one was to be compelled, for the king had commanded all the officials of the court to do according to each person's desire.
Queen Vashti also held a banquet for the women at the palace of King Ahasuerus.

On the seventh day, when the king's heart was merry with the power of the wine, he commanded the seven eunuchs — Mehuman, Biztha, Harbona, Bigtha, Abagtha, Zethar, and Carkas — who served before King Ahasuerus,
to bring Queen Vashti, wearing the royal crown, in before the king, so that the peoples and the officials might behold her beauty, for she had radiant loveliness.
But Queen Vashti refused to come according to the king's command that the eunuchs delivered. The king then became greatly enraged, and his fury blazed like fire within his heart.
Then the king spoke with the wise men learned in the ways of the times, for according to royal custom the king would consult those who knew law and judgment.

Those closest to him were Carshena, Shethar, Admatha, Tarshish, Meres, Marsena, and Memucan — the seven nobles of Persia and Media who regularly had audience with the king and held the highest positions in the kingdom.
The king asked, 'According to the law, what should be done with Queen Vashti, since she did not carry out the command of King Ahasuerus that the eunuchs delivered?'

Then Memucan spoke before the king and the officials, 'Lady Vashti the queen has done wrong not against the king alone, but against all the officials and all the peoples in every province of King Ahasuerus.
For this deed of the queen will spread to all the women, making them despise their husbands, as they say, 'King Ahasuerus commanded that Queen Vashti be brought in, but she would not come.'
This very day the noble women of Persia and Media who have heard of the queen's matter will speak likewise to all the king's officials, giving rise to endless scorn and indignation.

Therefore, if it pleases the king, let a royal decree go forth from him and be recorded in the laws of the Persians and Medes so that it cannot be changed, that Vashti shall not come before King Ahasuerus again, and let the king grant her position as queen to another who is better than she.
When the royal edict that the king proclaims is heard throughout his vast kingdom, all the women will give honor to their husbands, from the greatest to the least.'
This counsel was pleasing to the king and the officials, so the king acted according to the word of Memucan.

He sent royal letters to all the provinces of the king, to each province in its own script and to each people in its own language, that every man should be master in his own household and proclaim it in the language of his own people.
